title: Variational Autoencoders (VAE)
summary: Probabilistic autoencoder. Encoder outputs distribution parameters ($\mu, \sigma$), not a point. Sample from this distribution via reparameterization trick. Loss = reconstruction + KL divergence. Enables generation by sampling from the learned latent space.
key_insights: Standard autoencoder: $z = f(x)$ is a point. VAE: $z \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu(x), \sigma(x)^2)$ is a distribution.
Reparameterization trick: $z = \mu + \sigma \odot \epsilon$ where $\epsilon \sim \mathcal{N}(0, I)$. Makes sampling differentiable → gradients flow.
ELBO loss: $L = \text{reconstruction} + \beta \cdot D_{KL}(q(z|x) \| p(z))$. Balances fidelity vs latent space structure.
KL term pushes $q(z|x)$ toward prior $\mathcal{N}(0, I)$. Without it: latent space has gaps (can't sample new points).
$\beta$-VAE: increase $\beta > 1$ to force more disentangled latent dimensions (each dim captures one factor of variation).
Generation: sample $z \sim \mathcal{N}(0, I)$, decode. Unlike autoencoder, the latent space is smooth and structured.

derivations.title: Reparameterization trick — why it's necessary

steps.name: The problem
steps.math: $z \sim q_\phi(z|x)$. Sampling is NOT differentiable — $\partial z / \partial \phi$ is undefined. Can't backprop through random sampling.

steps.name: The trick
steps.math: Rewrite: $z = \mu_\phi(x) + \sigma_\phi(x) \odot \epsilon$. Now $z$ is a deterministic function of $\phi$ (plus fixed noise $\epsilon$). $\partial z / \partial \mu = I$, $\partial z / \partial \sigma = \epsilon$.

derivations.consequence: Gradients flow through the sampling operation. The randomness is in $\epsilon$ (which doesn't depend on $\phi$), not in the parameters.

derivations.title: ELBO derivation — why this loss maximizes data likelihood

steps.name: Start with log-likelihood
steps.math: $\log p(x) = \log \int p(x|z) p(z) dz$. Intractable for complex decoders.

steps.name: Introduce variational distribution
steps.math: $\log p(x) \geq \mathbb{E}_{q(z|x)}[\log p(x|z)] - D_{KL}(q(z|x) \| p(z))$. This is the ELBO (Evidence Lower BOund).

steps.name: Gap
steps.math: $\log p(x) - \text{ELBO} = D_{KL}(q(z|x) \| p(z|x)) \geq 0$. ELBO is tight when $q$ matches the true posterior.

derivations.consequence: Maximizing ELBO = maximizing a lower bound on data log-likelihood. Better $q$ (encoder) → tighter bound → better model.

related: DIFFUSION.BASIC — both are latent-variable generative models
